faq.title: What people often ask
faq.lead: Short, honest answers to the questions families bring in most often. Don't see yours below? Send your reports or ring our line — we'll respond personally within 24 hours.

callout.title: Still got a question?
callout.body: Send us your reports or call our line — a consultant will get back to you within 24 hours.
callout.whatsapp_prefill: Hello, I have a question for the SS Kidney Care team.

items.question: When should I see a nephrologist?
items.answer: If you have diabetes, high blood pressure, a family history of kidney disease, or repeated UTIs, a yearly kidney check is sensible — early damage rarely shows symptoms. If your reports already flag protein in urine or a rising creatinine, please come in sooner.

items.question: What should I bring on the first visit?
items.answer: Bring whatever reports you already have — blood tests (creatinine, urea, eGFR), urine routine, BP records, and a list of your current medicines all help. WhatsApp photos of older reports are fine. If you have none, that's alright; we'll start a fresh workup.

items.question: Can kidney damage be reversed?
items.answer: Early-stage damage can often be slowed, and sometimes partially reversed — especially when it's caused by uncontrolled sugar, blood pressure, or long-term pain medicines. Later stages can't be reversed, but with the right plan they can be managed comfortably for years.

items.question: How long does a dialysis session take, and how often will I need it?
items.answer: A standard hemodialysis session runs about four hours, usually two or three times a week. Most patients keep working or studying around it — we help you pick morning or evening slots that fit the rest of your day.

items.question: What can I eat while on dialysis?
items.answer: Less restrictive than people fear. Low salt, moderate protein, careful with fluids, and watchful with potassium-rich foods like bananas, coconut water and certain fruits. Our dietitian sits with you and your cook to plan around what's actually on your plate at home.

items.question: Is dialysis available at night or in an emergency?
items.answer: Yes. Our dialysis centre and renal ICU run 24×7. If your doctor calls ahead or you reach our emergency line, we can arrange an urgent session — including for visitors, travellers, and patients passing through Sambalpur.

items.question: Can I send my reports for a second opinion before coming in?
items.answer: Of course. WhatsApp or email your latest reports with a brief note on your symptoms — a consultant writes back within 24 hours with a sense of whether you need a visit, more tests, or just a follow-up call.

items.question: Do you accept insurance for cashless treatment?
items.answer: We're empanelled with most major insurers and TPAs for cashless treatment. Bring your card on the first visit and our front desk handles the paperwork. If your insurer isn't on our list, we'll help with reimbursement instead.
